summary: Spolsky highlights that Microsoft followed the approach he predicted months earlier, making Avalon available as standalone libraries for existing Windows versions rather than requiring users to upgrade to Longhorn. He frames this as a vindication of Raymond Chen's pragmatic philosophy over the marketing-driven approach of forcing OS upgrades. The post also briefly mentions work on discussion group software with a small but potentially significant feature allowing users to link their names to URLs.
key_points: Microsoft reversed course on making Avalon exclusive to Longhorn, confirming Spolsky's earlier prediction that there was no technical reason to bundle it with the new OS
The decision validates Raymond Chen's philosophy of backward compatibility and practical engineering over marketing-driven platform lock-in
Small features like letting users link their names to URLs can have outsized effects on community behavior and dynamics
money_quotes: There's no technical reason not to do this. But Microsoft needs to give you a reason to buy Longhorn.
It's a small feature that I'm hoping will have a big effect on the anthropology of the discussion group.
Like everything else, it's an experiment.
key_insight: When Microsoft decoupled Avalon from Longhorn, it proved that technical decisions driven by marketing rather than engineering inevitably get corrected.
